1:
"And these are the countries which the children of Israel inherited in the land of Canaan, which Eleazar the priest, and Joshua... and the heads of the fathers of the tribes... distributed for inheritance to them." The witnesses note the division of Canaan by Eleazar, Joshua, and the heads of the tribes. The witnesses note that Gill notes an exact account of these inheritances is given in the following chapters. The witnesses note that Henry notes the historian now turns to the disposal of the land west of Jordan. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the countries inherited — in Canaan; the Eleazar and Joshua — the priest and leader; the the heads of the tribes — the elders; the order and consent — all done decently and in order (1 Cor. 14:40).

The witnesses agree the verse opens the account of the western inheritance, divided by due authority — "Eleazar the priest, and Joshua... and the heads of the fathers": the dividing was committed not to one man's will but to the priest, the leader, and the tribal heads together, that all might be done with order and consent; the joining of priest, ruler, and elders shows the gravity and fairness of the work; it teaches that the affairs of God's people are to be ordered by lawful authority and common counsel, not by private will, "let all things be done decently and in order" (1 Cor. 14:40), the inheritance distributed by priest, leader, and heads.

So the witnesses make the verse the division: The witnesses note the the countries inherited: in Canaan. The witnesses note the Eleazar and Joshua: the priest and leader. The witnesses note the the heads of the tribes: the elders. The witnesses note the order and consent: all done decently and in order (1 Cor. 14:40). The expositors observe the the countries inherited: in Canaan. The expositors observe the Eleazar and Joshua: the priest and leader. The expositors observe the the heads of the tribes: the elders. The expositors observe the order and consent: all done decently and in order (1 Cor. 14:40). The believer is taught to mark the the countries inherited, the Eleazar and Joshua, the the heads of the tribes, the order and consent, and to wholly follow the LORD his God. The verse thus records that the western inheritance was divided by Eleazar, Joshua, and the tribal heads — the affairs of God's people ordered by lawful authority and common counsel, all done decently and in order (1 Cor. 14:40).
2:
"By lot was their inheritance, as the LORD commanded by the hand of Moses, for the nine tribes, and for the half tribe." The witnesses note the inheritance assigned by lot, as the LORD had commanded. The witnesses note that Gill notes the lot was used to prevent quarrels and strife, the disposing being of the Lord, and to show the agreement between the lot and the divine purpose. The witnesses note that it was for the nine tribes and the half tribe. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the by lot — their inheritance; the as the LORD commanded — by Moses; the to prevent strife — the disposing his; the God's appointment — the disposing is of the LORD (Prov. 16:33).

The witnesses agree the land was apportioned "by lot," that the dividing might be plainly God's and not man's: Gill notes the lot was used to prevent envy and strife among the tribes, the outcome resting wholly with the LORD; the casting of lots referred the whole to God's disposal, so that each tribe received its portion from his hand; it teaches that God himself appoints to his people their several lots, the believer to receive his place and portion as from the LORD, content with what divine wisdom assigns, "the lot is cast into the lap; but the whole disposing thereof is of the LORD" (Prov. 16:33), the inheritance divided by lot.

So the witnesses make the verse by lot: The witnesses note the by lot: their inheritance. The witnesses note the as the LORD commanded: by Moses. The witnesses note the to prevent strife: the disposing his. The witnesses note the God's appointment: the disposing is of the LORD (Prov. 16:33). The expositors observe the by lot: their inheritance. The expositors observe the as the LORD commanded: by Moses. The expositors observe the to prevent strife: the disposing his. The expositors observe the God's appointment: the disposing is of the LORD (Prov. 16:33). The believer is taught to mark the by lot, the as the LORD commanded, the to prevent strife, the God's appointment, and to wholly follow the LORD his God. The verse thus records that the inheritance was assigned by lot as the LORD commanded — the dividing plainly God's, preventing strife, each receiving his portion from the LORD's hand, the whole disposing of the lot his (Prov. 16:33).
3:
"For Moses had given the inheritance of two tribes and an half tribe on the other side Jordan: but unto the Levites he gave none inheritance among them." The witnesses note that the eastern tribes were already settled by Moses, and the Levites given no land. The witnesses note that Gill notes the two tribes of Gad and Reuben, and the half tribe of Manasseh, whose inheritance was already described. The witnesses note that to the Levites was given no inheritance among them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the two tribes and a half — settled by Moses; the on the other side Jordan — already provided; the the Levites none — no land; the God their portion — i am thy part and inheritance (Num. 18:20).

The witnesses agree the verse recalls the prior settlement and the exception of Levi: "Moses had given the inheritance of two tribes and an half... but unto the Levites he gave none"; the eastern tribes were already provided, and Levi, by God's appointment, was to have no land but the LORD himself; the noting of Levi's exception keeps before us the truth that this tribe's portion was of another kind; it teaches that God provides for his people in differing ways, some with earthly portions and some with himself for their inheritance, the minister's heritage being the LORD, "I am thy part and thine inheritance among the children of Israel" (Num. 18:20), Levi given no land among them.

So the witnesses make the verse Levi no land: The witnesses note the two tribes and a half: settled by Moses. The witnesses note the on the other side Jordan: already provided. The witnesses note the the Levites none: no land. The witnesses note the God their portion: I am thy part and inheritance (Num. 18:20). The expositors observe the two tribes and a half: settled by Moses. The expositors observe the on the other side Jordan: already provided. The expositors observe the the Levites none: no land. The expositors observe the God their portion: I am thy part and inheritance (Num. 18:20). The believer is taught to mark the two tribes and a half, the on the other side Jordan, the the Levites none, the God their portion, and to wholly follow the LORD his God. The verse thus records that the eastern tribes were settled and the Levites given no land — God providing for his people in differing ways, the minister's heritage the LORD himself, I am thy part and thine inheritance (Num. 18:20).
4:
"For the children of Joseph were two tribes, Manasseh and Ephraim: therefore they gave no part unto the Levites in the land, save cities to dwell in..." The witnesses note Joseph counted as two tribes, and the Levites given cities to dwell in with their suburbs. The witnesses note that Gill notes the birthright forfeited by Reuben was given to Joseph, who had the double portion of the firstborn, keeping the number of twelve. The witnesses note that the Levites had cities to dwell in, with suburbs for their cattle. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Joseph two tribes — the double portion; the the number kept — at twelve; the cities to the Levites — to dwell in; the ministers dispersed — the priest's lips keep knowledge (Mal. 2:7).

The witnesses agree the number of the tribes was kept whole by Joseph's double portion in Manasseh and Ephraim, while Levi received cities and suburbs rather than a tribal lot: "Joseph were two tribes... they gave no part unto the Levites... save cities to dwell in"; the double portion to Joseph, the birthright forfeited by Reuben, shows God's sovereign ordering of the blessing; and the Levites' scattered cities placed the servants of God among all the tribes; it teaches that God orders the inheritance of his people by his own wisdom, and disperses his ministers among them for their good, "the priest's lips should keep knowledge" (Mal. 2:7) throughout the land, Levi given cities among all.

So the witnesses make the verse Levi's cities: The witnesses note the Joseph two tribes: the double portion. The witnesses note the the number kept: at twelve. The witnesses note the cities to the Levites: to dwell in. The witnesses note the ministers dispersed: the priest's lips keep knowledge (Mal. 2:7). The expositors observe the Joseph two tribes: the double portion. The expositors observe the the number kept: at twelve. The expositors observe the cities to the Levites: to dwell in. The expositors observe the ministers dispersed: the priest's lips keep knowledge (Mal. 2:7). The believer is taught to mark the Joseph two tribes, the the number kept, the cities to the Levites, the ministers dispersed, and to wholly follow the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Joseph was two tribes and the Levites given cities among them — God ordering the inheritance by his wisdom, dispersing his ministers among the tribes for their good, the priest's lips keeping knowledge (Mal. 2:7).
5:
"As the LORD commanded Moses, so the children of Israel did, and they divided the land." The witnesses note the division carried out in exact obedience to the LORD's command. The witnesses note that Gill notes they did this by their heads and representatives, the high priest, chief ruler, and princes of the tribes. The witnesses note that Clarke sets the obedience to the LORD's command. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the as the LORD commanded — the rule given; the so they did — full obedience; the by their heads — the representatives; the all by his word — keep his commandments (1 John 5:3).

The witnesses agree the dividing was done in full obedience to God's word — "as the LORD commanded Moses, so the children of Israel did": the whole work was carried out by the rule he had given, not by their own devising; the careful conformity to the divine command marks the reverence with which they handled the inheritance; it teaches that the people of God are to order all their affairs by his word, doing as he has commanded and not otherwise, the obedient observance of his rule the mark of a faithful people, "this is the love of God, that we keep his commandments" (1 John 5:3), the land divided as the LORD commanded.

So the witnesses make the verse as the LORD commanded: The witnesses note the as the LORD commanded: the rule given. The witnesses note the so they did: full obedience. The witnesses note the by their heads: the representatives. The witnesses note the all by his word: keep his commandments (1 John 5:3). The expositors observe the as the LORD commanded: the rule given. The expositors observe the so they did: full obedience. The expositors observe the by their heads: the representatives. The expositors observe the all by his word: keep his commandments (1 John 5:3). The believer is taught to mark the as the LORD commanded, the so they did, the by their heads, the all by his word, and to wholly follow the LORD his God. The verse thus records that the land was divided in exact obedience to the LORD's command — all ordered by his word and not their own devising, the obedient observance of his rule the mark of a faithful people, that we keep his commandments (1 John 5:3).
6:
"Then the children of Judah came unto Joshua in Gilgal: and Caleb the son of Jephunneh the Kenezite said unto him, Thou knowest the thing that the LORD said... concerning me and thee in Kadeshbarnea." The witnesses note Caleb coming with the men of Judah to Joshua, recalling the LORD's word at Kadesh-barnea. The witnesses note that Gill notes these were some chief men of Judah accompanying Caleb for his honour, he being their prince. The witnesses note that Caleb recalled what the LORD had said of him and Joshua at Kadesh-barnea. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Caleb came — with Judah; the to Joshua — at Gilgal; the thou knowest the thing — the LORD said; the pleading the promise — hath he said, and shall he not do it? (Num. 23:19).

The witnesses agree the scene turns to Caleb, the aged hero of faith, coming to claim the promise made him long before: "Caleb... said... Thou knowest the thing that the LORD said... in Kadeshbarnea"; he comes pleading not his own merit but God's word, the old promise spoken when the spies returned; the appeal to what the LORD had said marks the ground of all true claims — the faithfulness of God to his word; it teaches that the believer holds God to his promises, pleading them in due time, sure that he who promised is faithful, "hath he said, and shall he not do it?" (Num. 23:19), Caleb claiming the word spoken at Kadesh.

So the witnesses make the verse Caleb came: The witnesses note the Caleb came: with Judah. The witnesses note the to Joshua: at Gilgal. The witnesses note the thou knowest the thing: the LORD said. The witnesses note the pleading the promise: hath he said, and shall he not do it? (Num. 23:19). The expositors observe the Caleb came: with Judah. The expositors observe the to Joshua: at Gilgal. The expositors observe the thou knowest the thing: the LORD said. The expositors observe the pleading the promise: hath he said, and shall he not do it? (Num. 23:19). The believer is taught to mark the Caleb came, the to Joshua, the thou knowest the thing, the pleading the promise, and to wholly follow the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Caleb came to Joshua recalling the LORD's word at Kadesh-barnea — pleading not his merit but God's promise, the believer holding God to his word, sure that he who promised is faithful (Num. 23:19).
7:
"Forty years old was I when Moses the servant of the LORD sent me from Kadeshbarnea to espy out the land; and I brought him word again as it was in mine heart." The witnesses note Caleb recalling his spying mission and his faithful, honest report. The witnesses note that Gill notes he and eleven others were sent, one from each tribe, of like age, to show they were men of years and judgment. The witnesses note that he brought word again as it was in his heart. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the forty years old — when sent; the to espy the land — the mission; the word again — his report; the honest and faithful — a true witness delivereth souls (Prov. 14:25).

The witnesses agree Caleb recalls his faithfulness as a spy, when he "brought... word again as it was in mine heart": where ten brought an evil report and discouraged the people, Caleb spoke the truth in faith, without fear or flattery; his honest, courageous report — given as his heart truly believed — stood against the unbelief of the many; it teaches that faithfulness speaks the truth according to conviction, not bending to the fear of men, the faithful witness keeping a good conscience, "a true witness delivereth souls" (Prov. 14:25), Caleb's report given as it was in his heart.

So the witnesses make the verse as it was in my heart: The witnesses note the forty years old: when sent. The witnesses note the to espy the land: the mission. The witnesses note the word again: his report. The witnesses note the honest and faithful: a true witness delivereth souls (Prov. 14:25). The expositors observe the forty years old: when sent. The expositors observe the to espy the land: the mission. The expositors observe the word again: his report. The expositors observe the honest and faithful: a true witness delivereth souls (Prov. 14:25). The believer is taught to mark the forty years old, the to espy the land, the word again, the honest and faithful, and to wholly follow the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Caleb brought back his report "as it was in mine heart" — faithfulness speaking the truth in faith against the unbelief of the many, a true witness keeping a good conscience (Prov. 14:25).
8:
"Nevertheless my brethren that went up with me made the heart of the people melt: but I wholly followed the LORD my God." The witnesses note Caleb's testimony — the other spies discouraged the people, but he wholly followed the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes the rest of the spies, excepting Joshua, who went up with him into the land. The witnesses note that Wesley reflects on Caleb wholly following the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the my brethren — made the heart melt; the the people discouraged — by unbelief; the but I — wholly followed; the undivided devotion — with him while ye be with him (2 Chr. 15:2).

The witnesses agree the heart of Caleb's testimony, twice repeated in this chapter, is his wholehearted faithfulness: "my brethren... made the heart of the people melt: but I wholly followed the LORD my God"; where the others spread unbelief and fear, Caleb gave himself fully to God, holding nothing back; the phrase "wholly followed" marks an entire, undivided devotion, the very thing God commends and rewards; it teaches that true faith follows the LORD with the whole heart, not by halves, and that such wholehearted following is the path of blessing, "the LORD... is with you, while ye be with him" (2 Chr. 15:2), Caleb wholly following his God.

So the witnesses make the verse wholly followed: The witnesses note the my brethren: made the heart melt. The witnesses note the the people discouraged: by unbelief. The witnesses note the but I: wholly followed. The witnesses note the undivided devotion: with him while ye be with him (2 Chr. 15:2). The expositors observe the my brethren: made the heart melt. The expositors observe the the people discouraged: by unbelief. The expositors observe the but I: wholly followed. The expositors observe the undivided devotion: with him while ye be with him (2 Chr. 15:2). The believer is taught to mark the my brethren, the the people discouraged, the but I, the undivided devotion, and to wholly follow the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Caleb wholly followed the LORD while the others discouraged the people — true faith following with the whole heart, the undivided devotion God commends and rewards, with him while ye be with him (2 Chr. 15:2).
9:
"And Moses sware on that day, saying, Surely the land whereon thy feet have trodden shall be thine inheritance... because thou hast wholly followed the LORD my God." The witnesses note the oath Moses sware, granting Caleb the land for his wholehearted following. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was the Lord that sware to what follows, Moses declaring the oath of the Lord. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the promise that the land he trod should be his inheritance. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Moses sware — the LORD's oath; the the land thy feet trod — thine inheritance; the because thou wholly followed — the reward; the faithfulness rewarded — faithful unto death, a crown of life (Rev. 2:10).

The witnesses agree the oath promised Caleb a sure inheritance as the reward of his faithfulness: "the land whereon thy feet have trodden shall be thine inheritance... because thou hast wholly followed the LORD"; the reason given is again his wholehearted following — the very ground of the promised blessing; Gill notes it was in truth the LORD's own oath; it teaches that God rewards wholehearted devotion with a sure portion, binding himself by oath to the faithful, the reward of perseverance certain, "be thou faithful unto death, and I will give thee a crown of life" (Rev. 2:10), Caleb's inheritance sworn for his following the LORD.

So the witnesses make the verse the oath sworn: The witnesses note the Moses sware: the LORD's oath. The witnesses note the the land thy feet trod: thine inheritance. The witnesses note the because thou wholly followed: the reward. The witnesses note the faithfulness rewarded: faithful unto death, a crown of life (Rev. 2:10). The expositors observe the Moses sware: the LORD's oath. The expositors observe the the land thy feet trod: thine inheritance. The expositors observe the because thou wholly followed: the reward. The expositors observe the faithfulness rewarded: faithful unto death, a crown of life (Rev. 2:10). The believer is taught to mark the Moses sware, the the land thy feet trod, the because thou wholly followed, the faithfulness rewarded, and to wholly follow the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Moses sware Caleb a sure inheritance because he wholly followed the LORD — God rewarding wholehearted devotion with a sure portion, binding himself by oath, faithful unto death and given the crown (Rev. 2:10).
10:
"And now, behold, the LORD hath kept me alive, as he said, these forty and five years... and now, lo, I am this day fourscore and five years old." The witnesses note Caleb owning that the LORD had kept him alive, as promised, these forty-five years. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Lord had upheld him in life and health, preserving him from many dangers in the wilderness, according to his promise. The witnesses note that he was now eighty-five years old. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD hath kept me — alive; the as he said — his promise; the forty-five years — through all dangers; the life God's gift — thou hast preserved my spirit (Job 10:12).

The witnesses agree Caleb ascribes his long preservation wholly to God's faithfulness — "the LORD hath kept me alive, as he said, these forty and five years": through all the dangers of the wilderness and the wars, God had kept his word, sparing Caleb while the unbelieving generation fell; the grateful acknowledgment marks a faith that traces every mercy to the LORD's promise; it teaches that the believer owes his very life and preservation to God's faithfulness, owning each year as a gift of his keeping, "thou hast... preserved my spirit" (Job 10:12), Caleb kept alive forty-five years as the LORD had said.

So the witnesses make the verse kept me alive: The witnesses note the the LORD hath kept me: alive. The witnesses note the as he said: his promise. The witnesses note the forty-five years: through all dangers. The witnesses note the life God's gift: thou hast preserved my spirit (Job 10:12). The expositors observe the the LORD hath kept me: alive. The expositors observe the as he said: his promise. The expositors observe the forty-five years: through all dangers. The expositors observe the life God's gift: thou hast preserved my spirit (Job 10:12). The believer is taught to mark the the LORD hath kept me, the as he said, the forty-five years, the life God's gift, and to wholly follow the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Caleb owned the LORD had kept him alive these forty-five years as promised — his life and preservation owed to God's faithfulness, every year a gift of his keeping, who preserves the spirit (Job 10:12).
11:
"As yet I am as strong this day as I was in the day that Moses sent me: as my strength was then, even so is my strength now, for war, both to go out, and to come in." The witnesses note Caleb's undiminished vigour at eighty-five, as strong for war as forty-five years before. The witnesses note that Gill notes he was as sound in mind, judgment, and memory, and as robust in body, as forty-five years before — a wonderful instance. The witnesses note that his strength was as much for war now as then. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the as strong this day — as then; the Moses sent me — forty-five years before; the for war — to go out and come in; the strength renewed — they that wait shall renew their strength (Isa. 40:31).

The witnesses agree the preservation of Caleb's full strength to old age was a signal token of God's blessing on his faithful servant: "as yet I am as strong this day... as my strength was then"; at eighty-five he was as fit for war as at forty, his vigour of mind and body kept whole; the marvel of his unimpaired strength shows how God can sustain his own in fullness of power for his service; it teaches that the LORD renews the strength of those who wait on him and follow him wholly, the faithful kept vigorous for their work, "they that wait upon the LORD shall renew their strength" (Isa. 40:31), Caleb as strong at eighty-five as at forty.

So the witnesses make the verse as strong this day: The witnesses note the as strong this day: as then. The witnesses note the Moses sent me: forty-five years before. The witnesses note the for war: to go out and come in. The witnesses note the strength renewed: they that wait shall renew their strength (Isa. 40:31). The expositors observe the as strong this day: as then. The expositors observe the Moses sent me: forty-five years before. The expositors observe the for war: to go out and come in. The expositors observe the strength renewed: they that wait shall renew their strength (Isa. 40:31). The believer is taught to mark the as strong this day, the Moses sent me, the for war, the strength renewed, and to wholly follow the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Caleb was as strong at eighty-five as at forty — a token of God's blessing, sustaining his servant in fullness of power, the LORD renewing the strength of those who wait on him (Isa. 40:31).
12:
"Now therefore give me this mountain, whereof the LORD spake in that day; for thou heardest in that day how the Anakims were there... if so be the LORD will be with me, then I shall be able to drive them out..." The witnesses note Caleb's bold request for the mountain where the giant Anakim dwelt. The witnesses note that Gill notes he asked not one city only but a large tract, the hill country where Hebron and Debir were. The witnesses note that Clarke sets his confidence that the LORD would be with him to drive out the Anakim. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the give me this mountain — the hardest place; the the Anakims were there — the giants; the if the LORD be with me — his confidence; the faith attempts great things — if God be for us, who against us (Rom. 8:31).

The witnesses agree Caleb's request is a model of bold and humble faith — he asks not for ease but for the hardest place, where the giants were: "give me this mountain... how the Anakims were there... if so be the LORD will be with me, then I shall be able to drive them out"; he chose the difficult inheritance, confident not in himself but in God's presence; the faith that seeks the stronghold of giants, relying on the LORD, is the faith that honours him; it teaches that true faith does not shrink from the hardest task but, trusting God's presence, attempts great things for him, "if God be for us, who can be against us?" (Rom. 8:31), Caleb asking for the mountain of the Anakim.

So the witnesses make the verse give me this mountain: The witnesses note the give me this mountain: the hardest place. The witnesses note the the Anakims were there: the giants. The witnesses note the if the LORD be with me: his confidence. The witnesses note the faith attempts great things: if God be for us, who against us (Rom. 8:31). The expositors observe the give me this mountain: the hardest place. The expositors observe the the Anakims were there: the giants. The expositors observe the if the LORD be with me: his confidence. The expositors observe the faith attempts great things: if God be for us, who against us (Rom. 8:31). The believer is taught to mark the give me this mountain, the the Anakims were there, the if the LORD be with me, the faith attempts great things, and to wholly follow the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Caleb boldly asked for the mountain of the giants — true faith seeking not ease but the hardest task, confident in God's presence, attempting great things for him, if God be for us, who can be against us (Rom. 8:31).
13:
"And Joshua blessed him, and gave unto Caleb the son of Jephunneh Hebron for an inheritance." The witnesses note Joshua blessing Caleb and granting him Hebron for his inheritance. The witnesses note that Gill notes Joshua blessed him by granting his request, congratulating him, and wishing him success against the giants. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Hebron given to Caleb. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Joshua blessed him — the faithful honoured; the gave him Hebron — the request granted; the for an inheritance — the promise kept; the faith rewarded — no good thing withheld from the upright (Ps. 84:11).

The witnesses agree Joshua honoured the faithful request, both blessing Caleb and granting him Hebron: "Joshua blessed him, and gave unto Caleb... Hebron"; the blessing joined to the gift shows the esteem in which the faithful old warrior was held, and the readiness of the leader to reward such faith; the granting of the very land Caleb asked confirms that God makes good his promises to those who wholly follow him; it teaches that faithfulness is honoured and its requests granted, the persevering believer receiving the blessing with the inheritance, "the LORD will give grace and glory: no good thing will he withhold from them that walk uprightly" (Ps. 84:11), Joshua blessing Caleb and giving him Hebron.

So the witnesses make the verse Joshua blessed him: The witnesses note the Joshua blessed him: the faithful honoured. The witnesses note the gave him Hebron: the request granted. The witnesses note the for an inheritance: the promise kept. The witnesses note the faith rewarded: no good thing withheld from the upright (Ps. 84:11). The expositors observe the Joshua blessed him: the faithful honoured. The expositors observe the gave him Hebron: the request granted. The expositors observe the for an inheritance: the promise kept. The expositors observe the faith rewarded: no good thing withheld from the upright (Ps. 84:11). The believer is taught to mark the Joshua blessed him, the gave him Hebron, the for an inheritance, the faith rewarded, and to wholly follow the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Joshua blessed Caleb and gave him Hebron — faithfulness honoured and its request granted, God making good his promise to those who wholly follow, no good thing withheld from the upright (Ps. 84:11).
14:
"Hebron therefore became the inheritance of Caleb... unto this day, because that he wholly followed the LORD God of Israel." The witnesses note Hebron becoming Caleb's lasting inheritance, because he wholly followed the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes "unto this day" means to the time of the writing of the book. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Hebron as Caleb's inheritance because he wholly followed the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Hebron the inheritance — of Caleb; the unto this day — a lasting portion; the because he wholly followed — the secret of blessing; the an enduring reward — he that doeth God's will abideth for ever (1 John 2:17).

The witnesses agree the reason for Caleb's lasting inheritance is given a third time — his wholehearted following: "Hebron... became the inheritance of Caleb... because that he wholly followed the LORD God of Israel"; the threefold repetition of this phrase in the chapter fastens it on the reader as the secret of his blessing; the abiding possession, held "unto this day," was the fruit of an undivided devotion that lasted a lifetime; it teaches that wholehearted, persevering following of God brings an enduring reward, the faithful inheriting a lasting portion, "he that doeth the will of God abideth for ever" (1 John 2:17), Hebron Caleb's because he wholly followed the LORD.

So the witnesses make the verse because he wholly followed: The witnesses note the Hebron the inheritance: of Caleb. The witnesses note the unto this day: a lasting portion. The witnesses note the because he wholly followed: the secret of blessing. The witnesses note the an enduring reward: he that doeth God's will abideth for ever (1 John 2:17). The expositors observe the Hebron the inheritance: of Caleb. The expositors observe the unto this day: a lasting portion. The expositors observe the because he wholly followed: the secret of blessing. The expositors observe the an enduring reward: he that doeth God's will abideth for ever (1 John 2:17). The verse thus records that Hebron became Caleb's lasting inheritance because he wholly followed the LORD — wholehearted, persevering devotion bringing an enduring reward, he that doeth the will of God abideth for ever (1 John 2:17).
15:
"And the name of Hebron before was Kirjatharba; which Arba was a great man among the Anakims. And the land had rest from war." The witnesses note Hebron's old name from Arba, greatest of the Anakim, and that the land had rest from war. The witnesses note that Gill notes Kirjath-arba, "the city of Arba," named from the greatest man among the Anakim. The witnesses note that the land had rest from war. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Kirjath-arba — the city of Arba; the the greatest Anakim — the giant's city; the given to Caleb — the faithful; the the land had rest — a rest remains for God's people (Heb. 4:9).

The witnesses agree the chapter closes with the city's former name — once "Kirjatharba," the city of the greatest of the giants — now given to Caleb, and with the note that "the land had rest from war": the stronghold of the mightiest Anakite passed into the hands of God's faithful servant, and the long warfare came to its rest; the closing word of rest marks the completion of the conquest's main work, a foreshadowing of the rest that remains for the people of God after their warfare; it teaches that faithful following leads at last to rest, the conflict ending in the promised peace, "there remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God" (Heb. 4:9), the city of the giant given to Caleb and the land at rest.

So the witnesses make the verse rest from war: The witnesses note the Kirjath-arba: the city of Arba. The witnesses note the the greatest Anakim: the giant's city. The witnesses note the given to Caleb: the faithful. The witnesses note the the land had rest: a rest remains for God's people (Heb. 4:9). The expositors observe the Kirjath-arba: the city of Arba. The expositors observe the the greatest Anakim: the giant's city. The expositors observe the given to Caleb: the faithful. The expositors observe the the land had rest: a rest remains for God's people (Heb. 4:9). The believer is taught to mark the Kirjath-arba, the the greatest Anakim, the given to Caleb, the the land had rest, and to wholly follow the LORD his God. The verse thus records that the giant's city became Caleb's and the land had rest from war — faithful following leading at last to rest, the conflict ending in peace, a rest remaining for the people of God (Heb. 4:9).
